threats.title: Staking Pool Share Dilution and Theft via Unadjusted Deposit Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: The AbsBoldStakingPool contract does not decrement user deposit balances when tokens are transferred out to create an assertion or edge. This allows an attacker to steal newly deposited funds from other users after their own stake has been slashed.
threats.investigation_rationale: Staking pools are designed to be trustless aggregators. If accounting does not reflect locked or slashed funds, the pool becomes a vehicle for stealing subsequent deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: depositBalance[msg.sender] is checked against amount, but does not account for tokens currently locked in the rollup/challenge manager.

preconditions: Attacker deposits funds into the pool
Pool creates an assertion or edge that ultimately loses and is slashed
Victim deposits new funds into the pool

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdrawFromPool relies solely on depositBalance without checking if the pool actually holds enough free tokens, allowing withdrawal of any tokens currently in the contract.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after a pool's stake is slashed and a new user deposits.

operational_questions: Can an attacker intentionally create a doomed assertion/edge via the pool?
Does the pool have any mechanism to write down deposit balances when slashed?
validation_requirements: Simulate a slashing event on the pool and attempt to withdraw newly deposited funds.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deposits minimum required stake into a new staking pool.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls createAssertion/createEdge with invalid data, guaranteeing a loss.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Stake is slashed by the protocol; pool token balance becomes 0.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim deposits tokens into the pool.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_5: Attacker immediately calls withdrawFromPool, draining the victim's tokens using their intact depositBalance.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires monitoring for new deposits into the compromised pool.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete loss of user funds deposited into staking pools.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Lost funds cannot be recovered; pool contract must be abandoned.

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - can be executed entirely on-chain.
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of ERC20 and staking pool mechanics.
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires front-running or immediate action after victim deposit.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Withdrawals from pools immediately following new deposits, especially after a slashing event.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Pool token balance dropping to zero while total deposit balances remain high.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Addresses creating doomed assertions via pools.

threats.title: Permanent Loss of First Edge Stake on Challenge Defeat
threats.hypothesis_description: The EdgeChallengeManager retains the stake of the first edge in a rival group within the contract. If this first edge loses the challenge, its stake cannot be refunded and is permanently locked, bypassing the excessStakeReceiver redistribution mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: Economic security relies on proper stake routing. If honest validators lose funds permanently due to a protocol routing bug (even when they are the first to challenge a malicious assertion), participation incentives are destroyed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address receiver = edgeAdded.hasRival ? excessStakeReceiver : address(this); st.safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, receiver, sa);

preconditions: A validator creates the first layer zero edge for a claim
The edge is subsequently challenged and loses the bisection game

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: refundStake requires the edge to be Confirmed. A losing edge is never Confirmed, so its stake remains in address(this) forever.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Occurs automatically upon the defeat of any first-created edge.

operational_questions: Is there any alternative administrative function to sweep stuck stakes from the EdgeChallengeManager?
Does the protocol intend for the first edge's stake to be burned rather than redistributed?
validation_requirements: Trace the lifecycle of a losing first edge's stake to confirm it remains permanently locked.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Honest validator creates the first layer zero edge to challenge it.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker creates a rival edge, sending their stake to excessStakeReceiver.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If the honest validator makes a mistake or loses due to a bug, their stake is permanently locked in the contract.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of validator funds; degrades economic incentives to be the first challenger.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires a contract upgrade to recover locked funds.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Accumulating stakeToken balance in the EdgeChallengeManager contract over time.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Validators complaining about unrecoverable stakes after losing a challenge.

threats.title: Premature Force Inclusion via Missing Timestamp Delay Check
threats.hypothesis_description: The forceInclusion function in SequencerInbox only verifies that the block-based delay has expired, entirely omitting the time-based delay check. On chains with variable block times, this allows attackers to force-include messages earlier than the intended wall-clock delay.
threats.investigation_rationale: Force inclusion is a critical censorship-resistance mechanism. If it can be triggered prematurely, it disrupts the sequencer's intended operational window and can cause L2 reorgs or MEV extraction opportunities.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) revert ForceIncludeBlockTooSoon(); // Missing equivalent check for l1BlockAndTime[1] + delaySeconds_

preconditions: Network experiences a period of faster-than-average block production
An attacker wishes to force-include a transaction before the sequencer's time window expires

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: The Error.sol file defines ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon, but it is never thrown in SequencerInbox.sol.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During periods of rapid block production on the host chain.

operational_questions: Does the host chain (e.g., Ethereum or Arbitrum) have sufficient block time variance to make this exploitable?
Was the omission of the timestamp check intentional to save gas?
validation_requirements: Verify if forceInclusion succeeds when block delay has passed but time delay has not.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker submits a transaction to the delayed inbox.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker monitors block production. If blocks are produced faster than the target rate, the block delay expires before the time delay.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls forceInclusion immediately after the block delay expires, bypassing the sequencer's remaining time window.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction is forced into the L2 state prematurely.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires precise timing based on L1 block production rates.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Sequencer loses its guaranteed time window to order transactions, potentially disrupting L2 MEV or operational batching.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be recovered; requires contract upgrade to fix.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - requires monitoring L1 block times and mempool.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: forceInclusion events occurring earlier than the configured delaySeconds.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sequencer failing to include messages that were force-included prematurely.

threats.title: Delay Buffer Replenishment Starvation via Frequent Batching
threats.hypothesis_description: The delay buffer replenishment calculation uses integer division that rounds down to zero for small elapsed block intervals. If the sequencer posts batches frequently, the buffer will never replenish, permanently reducing the protocol's tolerance for sequencer downtime.
threats.investigation_rationale: The delay buffer is designed to adapt to sequencer performance. If mathematical rounding prevents replenishment, the buffer degrades into a one-way ratchet, permanently weakening the protocol's operational resilience.
deployment_assumptions: replenishRateInBasis is configured to a standard value (e.g., 100-500)
Sequencer posts batches frequently (e.g., every 1-10 blocks)

proof_of_signal.pattern: buffer += (elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS; // Rounds to 0 if elapsed * rate < 10000

preconditions: Sequencer operates normally, posting batches with elapsed blocks such that elapsed * replenishRateInBasis < 10000

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Integer division without remainder carry-over means frequent small updates accumulate zero replenishment, while infrequent large updates would replenish normally.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on admin configuration of replenishRateInBasis.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous during normal high-frequency sequencer operation.

operational_questions: What is the intended replenishRateInBasis in production?
What is the average batch posting frequency of the sequencer?
validation_requirements: Calculate the exact rounding threshold based on production config and verify if normal operation causes starvation.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Sequencer experiences a brief outage, depleting the delay buffer.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Sequencer recovers and resumes high-frequency batch posting (e.g., every 5 blocks).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Because 5 * rate < 10000, replenishment is 0 per batch.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Buffer remains permanently depleted at the threshold, making future force inclusions much easier.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent reduction in sequencer delay tolerance; increased risk of malicious force inclusions.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin must manually reset the buffer config or sequencer must intentionally delay batches to force replenishment.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Buffer blocks remaining at threshold despite long periods of normal sequencer operation.

threats.title: Staking Pools Rendered Inoperable by Validator Whitelist Enforcement
threats.hypothesis_description: When the validator whitelist is enabled, EdgeChallengeManager enforces that msg.sender is a whitelisted validator. Because staking pools are deployed permissionlessly via CREATE2, they are not whitelisted by default, causing all pool-initiated edge creations to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Staking pools are intended to allow permissionless aggregation of funds. If the core protocol's whitelist mechanism blocks contract-based aggregators, the primary mechanism for retail participation is broken.
deployment_assumptions: Admin does not manually whitelist every dynamically created pool contract
threats.config_evidence: RollupAdminLogic.sol: setValidatorWhitelistDisabled controls the flag; EdgeChallengeManager.sol:134 enforces it on msg.sender.
preconditions: Validator whitelist is enabled on the Rollup
Users deposit funds into an EdgeStakingPool
The pool attempts to create an edge

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: The whitelist checks msg.sender, which is the pool contract, not the user initiating the transaction.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to have enabled the whitelist.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time the whitelist is active.

operational_questions: Is there a mechanism to automatically whitelist staking pools created by the official factory?
Are staking pools intended to be used only when the whitelist is disabled?
validation_requirements: Deploy a pool, enable whitelist, and attempt to call createEdge.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin enables validator whitelist for security reasons.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users continue to deploy and fund staking pools.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When a pool reaches the required threshold, any call to createEdge reverts.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User funds are stuck in the pool because the protocol move cannot be executed, and withdrawal may be blocked if the pool logic expects the move to complete.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete failure of the trustless staking pool ecosystem.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin must manually whitelist all pool contracts or disable the whitelist.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted createEdge transactions with NotValidator error.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Fully funded pools failing to create edges.

threats.title: Staker Funds Temporarily Locked During BOLD Upgrade Exceeding Cap
threats.hypothesis_description: The BOLDUpgradeAction limits automatic staker refunds to the first 50 stakers to avoid block gas limits. Any stakers beyond index 50 are left in the paused old rollup and must manually withdraw their funds using a patched user logic implementation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Major protocol upgrades should seamlessly migrate or refund all users. Leaving users behind requires out-of-band communication and relies on the patched logic functioning perfectly for edge cases.
deployment_assumptions: The old rollup has more than 50 active stakers at the time of the upgrade.
threats.config_evidence: BOLDUpgradeAction.sol:186 explicitly caps the loop at 50.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (stakerCount > 50) { stakerCount = 50; } for (uint64 i = 0; i < stakerCount; i++) { ... }

preconditions: More than 50 stakers exist in the old rollup

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hardcoded limit of 50 stakers means the upgrade is not fully atomic for all participants.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to execute the upgrade.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During and after the BOLD upgrade execution.

operational_questions: How many stakers are currently active on the target network?
Is the IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER guaranteed to allow withdrawal for stakers in all states (e.g., mid-challenge)?
validation_requirements: Review the patched old rollup user logic to ensure stakers > 50 can actually withdraw.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Stakers 51+ are left in the old paused rollup.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If the patched logic has flaws or if users are unaware, their funds remain locked indefinitely.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User friction and potential temporary/permanent lock of funds for a subset of validators.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires users to manually interact with deprecated contracts.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Stakers remaining in the old rollup post-upgrade.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Support tickets from validators unable to find their refunded stake.
